transcript: grizzly bears are among the world's largest land predators these animals are perfectly adapted to their environment with keen eyesight an acute sense of smell an incredible strength and power I've always wanted to get an insight into the behavior of these magnificent creatures. So when I had the opportunity to travel to Alaska to see them up close, I jumped at the chance. So I'm here in Katmai National Park and it's grizzly bear central and the salmon run is on so all the bears are concentrated on these rivers. The bears come here to feast and in places the river is actually pink with salmon. These rivers are an important part of the salmon's life cycle. They've swum all the way from the Pacific Ocean to reach their breeding grounds here. The bears follow the sockeye upstream feasting on this natural bounty. Salmon are a vital food source for them because they're rich in fats. Just one fish provides about four and a half thousand calories and they can eat over 20 a day. That's a staggering 100,000 calories 40 times what I eat. Triggered by hormonal changes, they're now in a phase of hyperphagia. They have an insatiable appetite but never feel full. This helps them build up fat reserves, which is very important. They've got to get through a long winter here, five months of hibernation. It's incredible. Just from here, I can see eight grizzly bears. It's just a dream to be here. Males are bulky with a wide head, whereas females are smaller with a narrow head. I've always been fascinated by how these bears hunt. And it's incredible to see it all happening up close. All of the bears have got different hunting techniques here. One of the male bears would hold his paw up just before he dashed into the water which would give us a real clue as to what he was going to do next. Whereas this mother bear we're watching here, she's just stood on the edge of the water and we're just waiting for her to dash in. Her cub seems to be waiting too. This cub is nearly two years old now and will soon have to fend for itself. So we're sat in the river here and we've literally got salmon bumping into us as they're travelling upstream. Because the salmon are so abundant, the bears can afford to be selective, preferring females for their nutrient-rich eggs and eating only the fattiest parts of the fish. The bears discard the rest but thankfully nothing goes to waste. Further downstream we come across a female with two cubs. Mother bears can have as many as four, but two is the norm. Bear cubs are born in winter, so these are about six months old. They spend the first months tucked away in their den, before emerging in spring. So this is their very first taste of an Alaskan summer. It's beautiful to watch them. and protect them from danger. The female has spotted two males coming up stream. It could be a threat. Male bears will sometimes kill the cubs to force the females into oestrus. She calls at the cubs, this time of year is so important. During the summer, adult bears must at least double their weight to survive the winter. What's even more incredible is the hard work of mother bears with newborn cubs. In their first year these youngsters will grow ten times their spring weight. It's been a real privilege to spend time out here with the bears and I can't wait to video. Don't forget to like, comment and subscribe to see more. Here's a taste of what you'll enjoy seeing on this channel.
